title: The Castle Ruins
content:
A happy day at Whitsuntide,
As soon ï¿½s the zun begun to vall,
We all strollï¿½d up the steep hill-zide
To Meldon, gret anï¿½ small;
Out where the Castle wall stood high
A-mwoldren to the zunny sky.

Anï¿½ there wiï¿½ Jenny took a stroll
Her youngest sister, Poll, so gay,
Bezide John Hind, ah! merry soul,
Anï¿½ mid her wedlock fay;
Anï¿½ at our zides did play anï¿½ run
My little maid anï¿½ smaller son.

Above the baten mwold upsprung
The driven doust, a-spreaden light,
Anï¿½ on the new-leavï¿½d thorn, a-hung,
Wer wool a-quivï¿½ren white;
Anï¿½ corn, a-sheenen bright, did bow,
On slopen Meldonï¿½s zunny brow.

There, down the roofless wall did glow
The zun upon the grassy vloor,
Anï¿½ weakly-wandren winds did blow,
Unhinderï¿½d by a door;
Anï¿½ smokeless now avore the zun
Did stanï¿½ the ivy-girded tun.

My bwoy did watch the dawsï¿½ bright wings
A-flappen vrom their ivy bowï¿½rs;
My wife did watch my maidï¿½s light springs,
Out here anï¿½ there vor flowï¿½rs;
And John did zee noo towï¿½rs, the place
Vor him had only Pollyï¿½s face.

Anï¿½ there, of all that pried about
The walls, I overlookï¿½d em best,
Anï¿½ what oï¿½ that? Why, I made out
Noo mwore than all the rest:
That there wer woonce the nest of zome
That wer a-gone avore we come.

When woonce above the tun the smoke
Did wreathy blue among the trees,
Anï¿½ down below, the liven voï¿½k
Did tweil as brisk as bees:
Or zit wiï¿½ weary knees, the while
The sky wer lightless to their tweil.
meaning:
About: William Barnes (22nd February 1801 - 7th October 1886) was an English writer, poet, minister, and philologist. 
He wrote over 800 poems, some in Dorset dialect and much other work including a comprehensive English grammar quoting from more than 70 different languages.
